page.title: ESG Regulatory Landscape
page.description: SFDR, EU Taxonomy, CSRD, ISSB, and the global push toward mandatory disclosure

items.title: EU NFRD Adopted
items.description: The Non-Financial Reporting Directive (2014/95/EU) required large public-interest companies (500+ employees) to disclose ESG information. Affected ~11,000 companies in the EU.

items.title: EU Action Plan on Sustainable Finance
items.description: The European Commission publishes its Action Plan, establishing three key pillars: (1) reorient capital flows, (2) mainstream sustainability in risk management, (3) foster transparency and long-termism. This led to SFDR and the Taxonomy Regulation.

items.title: SFDR & Taxonomy Regulation Effective
items.description: The **Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation (SFDR)** introduces Article 6/8/9 fund classification. The **EU Taxonomy Regulation** establishes the classification system for environmentally sustainable economic activities.

items.title: CSRD Adopted
items.description: The **Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)** replaces NFRD, expanding scope to ~50,000 companies (including non-EU companies with EU presence). Introduces mandatory **ESRS** standards and limited assurance.

items.title: ISSB S1 & S2 Published
items.description: The **International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB)** issues IFRS S1 (General Requirements) and IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures), marking the first global baseline for sustainability reporting. Built on TCFD, SASB, CDSB, and IIRC.

items.title: Implementation Phase
items.description: CSRD applies to first wave (previously under NFRD) from FY2024. SFDR Level 2 RTS (Regulatory Technical Standards) in effect. SEC climate rule (pending litigation). UK SDR, California climate bills (SB 253, SB 261) take effect. Global ISSB adoption begins in jurisdictions.

tabs.label: SFDR
tabs.content:
The **Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation (SFDR)** aims to prevent greenwashing in financial products.

- **Article 6:** Products that do not integrate ESG (no sustainability promotion).
- **Article 8:** Products that **promote** environmental or social characteristics (light green).
- **Article 9:** Products with **sustainable investment** as their objective (dark green).
- **PAI (Principal Adverse Impacts):** Mandatory disclosure of negative sustainability impacts at entity and product level.
- **Level 2 RTS:** Detailed reporting templates (2023+).
- **Impact:** Strong market migration from Art. 6 to Art. 8/9. Heavy scrutiny from regulators re: greenwashing.

tabs.label: EU Taxonomy
tabs.content:
The **EU Taxonomy Regulation** (Regulation 2020/852) defines environmentally sustainable activities.

- **6 Environmental Objectives:**
  1. Climate change mitigation
  2. Climate change adaptation
  3. Sustainable use of water/marine resources
  4. Circular economy
  5. Pollution prevention
  6. Biodiversity & ecosystems
- **DNSH:** Do No Significant Harm to any other objective.
- **Substantial Contribution:** The activity must make a substantive contribution to at least one objective.
- **Minimum Safeguards:** Social safeguards (OECD guidelines, UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights).
- **Disclosure:** Financial and non-financial companies must report taxonomy alignment (% of revenue, CapEx, OpEx aligned).

tabs.label: CSRD
tabs.content:
The **Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)** transforms EU sustainability reporting.

- **Scope:** ~50,000 companies (all large + listed SMEs). Non-EU companies with >EUR 150M EU turnover.
- **Standards:** Mandatory **European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS)** developed by EFRAG.
- **Double Materiality:** Mandatory for all reporters.
- **Assurance:** Limited assurance (evolving to reasonable assurance by 2028+).
- **Format:** Digital tagging (XBRL) for machine-readability.
- **Phased timeline:**
  - FY2024: Companies already subject to NFRD
  - FY2025: Large companies not subject to NFRD
  - FY2026: Listed SMEs (opt-out until 2028)

tabs.label: ISSB
tabs.content:
The **International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB)** was established by the IFRS Foundation in 2021 to create a global baseline.

- **IFRS S1:** General Requirements for Disclosure of Sustainability-related Financial Information.
- **IFRS S2:** Climate-related Disclosures (incorporates TCFD recommendations).
- **Architecture:** SASB standards incorporated as industry-specific guidance.
- **Global Baseline Ambition:** ISSB standards are designed as the foundation that jurisdictions (UK, Japan, Australia, Canada, Brazil) can build upon with additional requirements.
- **Early Adoption:** Several jurisdictions already moving to adopt or align with ISSB from 2024-2026.

items.heading: European Union (SFDR + CSRD + Taxonomy)
items.content:
The EU is the most advanced regulatory bloc, with an interconnected framework:
- **SFDR:** All financial market participants must classify funds and disclose PAI.
- **CSRD:** All large companies must report under ESRS using double materiality.
- **EU Taxonomy:** Revenue, CapEx, and OpEx alignment must be disclosed.
- **EU Green Bond Standard:** Voluntary standard for green bonds (2024+).
- **Enforcement:** Heavy regulator focus on greenwashing (ESMA, EIOPA, EBA statements).

items.heading: United Kingdom (UK SDR, TCFD)
items.content:
The UK has maintained momentum post-Brexit with its own framework:
- **UK SDR (Sustainability Disclosure Requirements):** FCA rules for asset managers, similar to SFDR but with clearer 'sustainability focus' categories.
- **TCFD-aligned disclosure:** Mandatory for premium-listed companies, banks, insurers, and asset managers.
- **UK Green Taxonomy:** Under development, aligned with EU Taxonomy but adapted for UK markets.
- **Transition Plan Taskforce (TPT):** Guidance for corporate climate transition plans.

items.heading: United States (SEC, California)
items.content:
The US regulatory landscape is evolving rapidly but fragmented:
- **SEC Climate Rule (2024):** Mandatory Scope 1/2 GHG disclosure for large filers. Scope 3 conditional on materiality. Stayed by courts pending litigation.
- **California SB 253 (Climate Corporate Data Accountability Act):** Mandatory Scope 1, 2, and 3 disclosure for companies >$1B revenue operating in California.
- **California SB 261 (Climate-Related Financial Risk Act):** Risk disclosure for companies >$500M revenue.
- **State-level vs. Federal:** California's rules may become the de facto national standard.

items.heading: Asia-Pacific (Japan, Singapore, Hong Kong, Australia)
items.content:
APAC markets are rapidly adopting sustainability reporting:
- **Japan:** JSCC mandates TCFD-aligned disclosure for TSE Prime Market from FY2025. Japan Stewardship Code.
- **Singapore:** SGX-listed companies must report under ISSB-aligned CSRD-comparable framework from FY2025.
- **Hong Kong:** HKEX mandates ESG reporting with increasing rigor (board oversight, climate metrics).
- **Australia:** AASB developing Australian Sustainability Reporting Standards (ASSRS) based on ISSB. Mandatory from FY2025.
- **China:** Three-year ESG disclosure mandate for listed companies from 2024.

items.heading: Emerging Markets (Brazil, India, South Africa)
items.content:
Emerging markets are leapfrogging with comprehensive frameworks:
- **Brazil:** CVM mandates ESG or sustainability reporting for listed companies from 2023 (IFRS/ISSB-aligned).
- **India:** SEBI's BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting) mandates ESG disclosure for top 1,000 listed companies. Value chain reporting from FY2024-25.
- **South Africa:** JSE-listed companies must report on King IV Code provisions and integrated reporting. Strong early adoption of ISSB.

pairs.right: Article 8 fund classification for products promoting ESG characteristics
Substantial contribution criteria and DNSH for 6 environmental objectives
Mandatory ESRS compliance with double materiality and limited assurance
General sustainability-related financial disclosures as a global baseline
Mandatory Scope 1/2 GHG disclosure for large US-listed filers

questions.question: Under SFDR, which article classification applies to funds that have sustainable investment as their explicit objective?
questions.explanation: **Article 9** (dark green) funds must have sustainable investment as their objective. Article 8 (light green) funds merely *promote* ESG characteristics. Article 6 funds do not integrate sustainability.
optionExplanations: Article 6 applies to products not integrating any ESG factors.
Article 8 covers products that promote ESG but do not have it as their core objective.
Correct! Article 9 is the dark green category for funds with explicit sustainable investment objectives.
There is no Article 10 in SFDR's classification system.

questions.question: What level of assurance is required under the CSRD for sustainability reporting?
options: Limited assurance initially, evolving to reasonable assurance
Only third-party review without stated assurance level
questions.explanation: CSRD requires **limited assurance** initially, with a planned transition to **reasonable assurance** (similar to financial audit) by 2028+ after the Commission assesses feasibility.
optionExplanations: CSRD does require assurance -- limited assurance initially.
Reasonable assurance is the long-term goal, not the starting point.
Correct! Limited assurance (initially) evolving to reasonable assurance is the CSRD timeline.
CSRD mandates a specific assurance standard, not just any review.

questions.question: What is the relationship between ISSB and TCFD?
options: ISSB replaced TCFD and fully incorporated its recommendations into IFRS S2
ISSB and TCFD are competing frameworks with no overlap
ISSB covers only social topics; TCFD covers climate
questions.explanation: The **ISSB** has fully incorporated **TCFD** recommendations into IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures). Reporting under IFRS S2 satisfies TCFD reporting obligations. The ISSB has effectively superseded TCFD at the international level.
optionExplanations: Correct! ISSB incorporated TCFD and the FSB has transitioned TCFD monitoring to IFRS.
ISSB was built on TCFD and SASB, incorporating rather than competing with them.
TCFD predates ISSB and was incorporated into it, not reversed.
IFRS S2 (climate) covers what TCFD covered; S1 covers general sustainability.

questions.question: How many environmental objectives does the EU Taxonomy Regulation define?
questions.explanation: The EU Taxonomy defines **6 environmental objectives**: (1) Climate change mitigation, (2) Climate change adaptation, (3) Sustainable use of water/marine resources, (4) Circular economy, (5) Pollution prevention, (6) Biodiversity and ecosystems.
optionExplanations: Correct! The six objectives cover climate, water, circular economy, pollution, and biodiversity.
